Explore the libnn project at /home/nick/work/libnn_0421 to map out infrastructure I need for a verification task. I want to verify two external tools end-to-end on a libnn function:

1. QEMU hot loop detector at /local/nick/qemu_v5/scripts/hotloops.sh (TCG plugin, prints top loops as `iters | start_pc -> end_pc | function+offset`). Important: ELF must be built WITHOUT `-g` so the user can pick from the table by hand.
2. vsim Kanata loop-window dump (vsim flags: --kanata file --kanata-loop-start PC --kanata-loop-end PC --kanata-loop-skip N --kanata-loop-record X). Doc lives at /local/nick/vsim/docs/KANATA_LOOP_WINDOW.md.

Tell me, with file paths and brief notes (no full file contents):

A) The /optimize skill: where it lives under .claude/skills/optimize/, what its FSM phases are, where it currently runs QEMU and vsim (if anywhere), and whether it has any existing hot-loop or Kanata helpers. Also note .claude/skills/hot-inner-kernel-extractor/ since it's adjacent.

B) How to BUILD a single libnn function as a runnable RISC-V ELF that QEMU can execute and vsim can simulate. Likely entry points: project/ax45mpv/, build.sh, test_perf.sh, qemu-validate/build_libnn.sh, qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh. I want to know:
   - Which script produces a per-function ELF (the perf test, not the unit test).
   - Where `-g` is currently injected, and how to override CFLAGS to drop it.
   - Whether vsim runs the same ELF format as QEMU (semihosting/baremetal).

C) Andes QEMU specifics: which qemu binary is used (the qemu-validate scripts vs /local/nick/qemu_v5/), whether the hotloops plugin .so has been built under /local/nick/qemu_v5/build, and whether plugins work with Andes-extension binaries (the libnn ELFs use vector + Andes vd4dots etc.).

D) vsim entry point: where the vsim binary lives, an example invocation already used in this repo (grep for `vsim` in skills and scripts), and whether the project has a working baremetal launch wrapper for AX45MPV.

E) Pick 1 candidate function from ROADMAP.md rows 1-4 (already completed/baselined) that is small enough to verify quickly. Note its perf-test ELF path or how to produce one.

Report under 400 words with paths and one-liners. Don't dump file contents.

2	name: optimize
3	description: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
4	argument-hint: [validate|function_name|status|reset]
5	disable-model-invocation: false
6	allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, Agent
7	---
8	
9	# libnn RVV Optimization Skill
10	
11	Fully autonomous optimization loop. Target (VPU, toolchain, FPGA, CFLAGS) is configured in `.claude/skills/optimize/config.toml`. State persisted in the `state_file` path set there (default: `.claude/skills/optimize/state.json`).
12	
13	## Current State
14	
15	!`LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel); SKILL_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize"; source "${SKILL_DIR}/script/parse_config.sh" 2>/dev/null; cat "${CFG_STATE_FILE:-${SKILL_DIR}/state.json}" 2>/dev/null || echo '{"step":"idle","function":"","round":0,"history":[]}'`
16	
17	## Commands
18	
19	- `/optimize validate` — Run config.toml validation (Step 0) only
20	- `/optimize <function_name>` — Start optimizing a function (validates first, then loops until flat land)
21	- `/optimize status` — Show current state
22	- `/optimize reset` — Reset to idle
23	
24	Arguments received: `$ARGUMENTS`
25	
26	## Finite State Machine
27	
28	```
29	idle → validate → select → analyze ──→ implement → verify ──→ measure ──→ learn ─┐
30	          |         ↑  |         ↑          |  |                                  |
31	          |         |  |         └──────────┘  |                                  |
32	          |         |  |      (test fail → fix)|                                  |
33	          |         |  |                       └→ analyze                         |
34	          |         |  |                (3 retries exhausted)                     |
35	          |         |  └→ idle (no opportunity found)                             |
36	          |         |                                                             |
37	          |         └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
38	          |           (loop with new insights + assembly analysis)
39	          └→ idle (config validation failed)
40	
41	Exit conditions:
42	  - validate fails → idle (fix config.toml, retry)
43	  - analyze finds no optimization opportunity → idle
44	  - max [limits].max_rounds per function → idle (5 by default; see config.toml)
45	```
46	
47	The loop is the core mechanic: after `learn`, go back to `analyze` with fresh insights from FPGA measurements, updated assembly, and recomputed metrics. The verify/measure loop provides real feedback.
48	
49	---
50	
51	## Build Scripts Reference
52	
53	All scripts live under the skill itself at `.claude/skills/optimize/script/`. They each source `parse_config.sh` to pull toolchain paths, CFLAGS, QEMU/FPGA targets, and work dir from `config.toml` — no hardcoded values. Always invoke with `/usr/bin/bash`.
54	
55	**Shell-variable convention for every bash block in this skill:**
56	```bash
57	LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
58	SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
59	```
60	When you need config values (work dir, toolchain, etc.) inside a bash block, `source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"` and use the exported `CFG_*` variables (e.g. `${CFG_WORK_DIR}`, `${CFG_CC}`, `${CFG_CPU}`).
61	
62	| Script | Purpose | Time |
63	|--------|---------|------|
64	| `validate_config.py` | 7-step fail-fast config validation (toolchain, QEMU, FPGA .inc parse, FPGA TCP, GDB run). Used by `validate` state. | ~10s |
65	| `build_libnn.sh` | Full rebuild of libnn.a (~400 files) using `${CFG_CPU}`. Use ONLY for initial build in `select` step. | ~30s |
66	| `rebuild_single.sh <src.c>` | Recompile single .c with `${CFG_CFLAGS_BASE}`, update libnn.a + objdump. Use for ALL rebuilds after `implement`. | ~0.6s |
67	| `rebuild_single.sh -E <src.c>` | Preprocess only (same CFLAGS). Output: `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/<basename>.i`. Use in `analyze` to see which `#ifdef` branches survive. | ~0.3s |
68	| `run_qemu_test.sh <name>` | Build test binary + run on QEMU with fd-9 strace capture + parse pass/fail. Uses `${CFG_QEMU_*}`. Use in `verify`. | ~2s |
69	| `run_fpga_test.sh <name>` | FPGA cycle count measurement via GDB. Uses `${CFG_FPGA_*}`. Use in `select` baseline + `measure`. | ~30s |
70	| `emit_trace.sh <sub>`    | Structured trace capture (Phase 0 meta-harness). Subcommands: `init`, `step`, `timing`, `fail`. Writes artifacts under `trace_corpus/<fn>/round_<N>/`. Auto-reads fn/round from state.json. | <1s |
71	
72	---
73	
74	## Trace Emission (Phase 0 meta-harness)
75	
76	After each reasoning step produces output, the main agent writes a structured
77	trace artifact to `trace_corpus/<fn>/round_<N>/` via `${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh`.
78	These artifacts are the input to the outer meta-loop's harness-critic (future phase).
79	Trace emission does NOT change FSM behavior.
80	
81	**Per-step artifacts:**
82	
83	| Step | Artifact | Contents |
84	|------|----------|----------|
85	| analyze   | `analyze_agent.md`    | Subagent hypothesis, ranked ideas, AI/reuse metrics |
86	| implement | `implement_agent.md`  | Subagent's description of the exact code change |
87	| verify    | `verify_result.json`  | `{"result": "PASS\|FAIL\|COMPILE_ERROR\|TIMEOUT\|UNKNOWN", "sub_tests": N, "stderr": "..."}` |
88	| measure   | `measure_result.json` | `{"cycles": N, "instrs": M, "ipc": X, "speedup": Y}` |
89	| learn     | `learn_agent.md`      | Lessons written to wiki this round (summary) |
90	| (any)     | `fail_reason.md`      | Tagged failure class: `compile_error`, `test_fail`, `regression`, `timeout`, `unknown` |
91	
92	Timing (wall-clock per step) is appended to `step_timings.json` automatically by
93	the state-transition block — no explicit call needed.
94	
95	**Emission pattern** (auto-mode reads fn/round from state.json; no flags needed):
96	
97	```bash
98	LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
99	SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
100	# Write payload to a temp file, then emit:
101	cat > /tmp/trace_payload <<'EOF'
102	<content>
103	EOF
104	/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step <step_name> /tmp/trace_payload
105	# Or on failure:
106	/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh fail <tag> /tmp/error_stderr
107	```
108	
109	No trace is emitted from `validate` or `select` — trace lifecycle begins at
110	`analyze` (which runs in a round context).
111	
112	---
113	
114	## State: `idle`
115	
116	No optimization in progress.
117	
118	Routing:
119	- `/optimize validate` → `validate` (run 7-step check, then return to `idle`)
120	- `/optimize <function_name>` → `validate` first (cache-aware); on PASS, continue to `select`; on FAIL, stay `idle`
121	- `/optimize status` → report current state, stay `idle`
122	- `/optimize reset` → clear state, stay `idle`
123	
124	---
125	
126	## State: `validate`
127	
128	Check that `config.toml` points at a working toolchain, reachable QEMU, parseable FPGA .inc, and live FPGA board BEFORE any optimization work starts. Failing fast here saves a lot of pain later.
129	
130	1. Cache check (skip re-validation if nothing has changed):
131	   ```bash
132	   LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
133	   SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
134	   source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
135	   # Skip if last validated within [limits].validate_cache_hours AND
136	   # config.toml + .inc mtimes are not newer than state.validated_at
137	   ```
138	   If cached validation is still fresh, skip to step 4.
139	
140	2. Run the 7-step validator:
141	   ```bash
142	   python3 "${SCRIPT_DIR}/validate_config.py"
143	   ```
144	   The script prints `[OK]` / `[FAIL]` per step and exits non-zero on the first failure. The 7 steps are:
145	   1. Load `config.toml` — all required sections/keys present
146	   2. Toolchain — `${CFG_TOOLCHAIN_BIN}` exists, `${CFG_CC}` accepts Andes flags
147	   3. Compile hello world — produces valid RISC-V ELF
148	   4. QEMU — `${CFG_QEMU_BIN}` runs hello.adx (strace-captured semihosting)
149	   5. FPGA .inc parse — `${CFG_FPGA_CONFIG}` parses (VLEN, caches, ISA features)
150	   6. FPGA TCP — connect to `${CFG_FPGA_HOST}:${CFG_FPGA_PORT}` (5s timeout)

1	---
2	name: hot-inner-kernel-extractor
3	description: Extract a libnn function's hot inner-most loop into a standalone, mcycle-wrapped RISC-V assembly harness so it can be measured on a cycle-accurate Verilator simulator (or any cycle counter) without running the whole function. Use this skill whenever the user wants to study pipeline behavior of a libnn kernel, profile inner-loop cycles, build a microbenchmark from an existing function, prepare a Verilator workload, isolate a hot loop body, or asks about "extracting", "isolating", "harnessing", "microbenchmark", "Verilator timeview", "pipeline timeview", "steady state", or "inner kernel" of any libnn / RVV function. The skill identifies the hottest translation block via QEMU's hotblocks plugin, walks objdump to find the innermost backward branch, computes live-in registers, and emits a compilable .S harness that runs the body N times under csrr mcycle.
4	allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, Agent
5	---
6	
7	# hot-inner-kernel-extractor
8	
9	Produces a self-contained, cycle-measurable assembly harness from a libnn
10	function's hot inner loop. Designed for cases where running the full function
11	on a cycle-accurate simulator (Verilator at ~9 kHz, RTL emulators, etc.) is
12	too slow to reach steady state -- by extracting just the inner kernel and
13	wrapping it in a counted measurement loop, you get pipeline-level cycle data
14	in seconds instead of hours.
15	
16	## When to use
17	
18	- User wants to profile pipeline behavior of an existing libnn kernel on
19	  Verilator and the full function is too slow to simulate.
20	- User wants a microbenchmark of a specific GEMM/conv/elementwise inner loop
21	  for cycle-accurate analysis or RTL bring-up.
22	- User asks "what's the inner kernel of `<fn>`" or "where does `<fn>` spend
23	  its cycles" -- the skill answers both, with a runnable artifact.
24	
25	## When NOT to use
26	
27	- For the FPGA cycle ground truth used by the `/optimize` loop -- that's
28	  whole-function measurement and is already automated by `/optimize`.
29	- For correctness debugging -- this skill produces a harness that exercises
30	  only the hot loop in isolation and does NOT verify outputs against golden.
31	
32	## Quick start
33	
34	Pick a target depending on what you want to measure:
35	
36	```bash
37	LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
38	SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/hot-inner-kernel-extractor/script"
39	
40	# FPGA: ground truth including cache/memory effects (the libnn perf default).
41	/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/extract_kernel.sh" <fn> --target fpga
42	/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_kernel_fpga.sh" <fn>
43	
44	# vsim (Verilator): pipeline-only cycles, no cache/memory model.
45	/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/extract_kernel.sh" <fn> --target vsim
46	/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_kernel_vsim.sh" <fn>
47	
48	# Standalone (default): bare-metal harness.s for custom Verilator testbench.
49	/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/extract_kernel.sh" <fn>
50	```
51	
52	**`--iters` defaults to 100.** That's the sweet spot we measured on the
53	AX45MPV conv kernel: 0.9% error vs steady-state, ~4s vsim runtime, well
54	below the 1-3% wins typical tuning rounds chase. Going to N=1000 cuts
55	error to 0.09% but takes 3x longer per measurement (12s on vsim) -- not
56	worth it during iteration. Use `--iters 1000` only for final baseline
57	reports. Going below 100 amplifies per-call overhead noise (N=10 has
58	8.8% error -- enough to mask real wins). The math: apparent cyc/iter =
59	true_cyc + per_call_overhead/N, so error scales 1/N while runtime scales
60	N -- diminishing returns kick in past N=100.
61	
62	The two simulators answer different questions:
63	- **FPGA** -- "how many cycles does this take in the real system?"
64	  Includes I-cache misses, D-cache misses, DRAM stalls. Same number the
65	  /optimize loop optimizes against.
66	- **vsim** -- "how many cycles does this take if memory were free?"
67	  Pipeline-only, useful for tuning instruction scheduling without
68	  memory effects masking your changes.
69	
70	Outputs land under `<skill>/work/<function_name>/`:
71	- `analysis.json` -- machine-readable: hot block PC, loop boundaries,
72	  live-ins, prologue, top hotblocks, etc.
73	- `analysis.md`   -- human-readable report with the loop body, live-in
74	  list, prologue, and a heuristic-init harness explainer
75	- `harness.s` (`--target standalone`) -- bare-metal `_start`, internal
76	  `csrr mcycle` reads, `cycle_total` symbol for memory scrape
77	- `kernel.s` + `kernel.c` (`--target fpga`) -- callable
78	  `extracted_kernel()` + a libnn `nds_pfcounter.h` wrapper. Run via
79	  `run_kernel_fpga.sh`. Cycles match the FPGA perf-test harness.
80	- `kernel.s` + `kernel.c` (`--target vsim`) -- same `extracted_kernel()`,
81	  different wrapper using raw `csrr mcycle/minstret` (vsim is bare-metal,
82	  no nds_pfcounter). Output line format matches FPGA verbatim
83	  (`"The cycle count is N"`, `"The inst count is N"`) so the grep
84	  pipeline is shared.
85	
86	## Pipeline (FSM-style, but executed straight through)
87	
88	```
89	resolve_fn -> ensure_libnn_built -> profile_qemu_hotblocks
90	    -> analyze_objdump_loop -> emit_harness -> write_report
91	```
92	
93	The skill is one-shot: each invocation produces a fresh artifact set. No
94	state persists across calls (unlike the `/optimize` skill, which has an FSM
95	across rounds).
96	
97	### Step 1: resolve function
98	
99	Given `<function_name>`, resolve to:
100	- The unit test name (strip `riscv_nn_` prefix if present)

4	#
5	# No fast-forward needed — the ELF starts executing the kernel immediately.
6	# Compiles a .S file, runs it in AndesCycle, logs pipeline from cycle 0.
7	#
8	# Usage:
9	#   run_vsim_kernel.sh <kern.S> [--log-cycles N]
10	#
11	# Example:
12	#   run_vsim_kernel.sh vsim-bench/kern_maxpool_HWC_s8.S --log-cycles 2000
13	#
14	# Output:
15	#   qemu-validate/work/vsim_<basename>.kanata.log — pipeline log
16	################################################################################
17	set -uo pipefail
18	
19	SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
20	WORK_DIR="${SCRIPT_DIR}/work"
21	VSIM_DIR="/local/nick/vsim-workspace/vsim"
22	
23	# Parse args
24	LOG_CYCLES=2000
25	KERN_SRC=""
26	
27	while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
28	    case "$1" in
29	        --log-cycles) LOG_CYCLES="$2"; shift 2 ;;
30	        -*)           echo "Unknown option: $1" >&2; exit 1 ;;
31	        *)            KERN_SRC="$1"; shift ;;
32	    esac
33	done
34	
35	if [ -z "${KERN_SRC}" ]; then

40	BASENAME="$(basename "${KERN_SRC}" .S)"
41	ELF="${WORK_DIR}/${BASENAME}.elf"
42	OBJDUMP_FILE="${WORK_DIR}/${BASENAME}.objdump"
43	KANATA_LOG="${WORK_DIR}/vsim_${BASENAME}.kanata.log"
44	
45	mkdir -p "${WORK_DIR}"
46	
47	# Compile
48	echo "[compile] ${KERN_SRC}"
49	riscv64-elf-gcc -march=rv64gcv -mabi=lp64d -nostdlib \
50	    -o "${ELF}" "${KERN_SRC}" -Wl,-Ttext=0x10000 || exit 1
51	
52	riscv64-elf-objdump -d "${ELF}" > "${OBJDUMP_FILE}"
53	echo "[objdump] ${OBJDUMP_FILE}"
54	
55	# Run vsim — no fast-forward, log from cycle 0
56	echo "[vsim] Logging ${LOG_CYCLES} cycles from start"
57	
58	ln -sfn "${VSIM_DIR}/tools" /tmp/vsim
59	PYTHONPATH="/tmp:${VSIM_DIR}/tools" python3 - "${ELF}" "${OBJDUMP_FILE}" "${KANATA_LOG}" "${LOG_CYCLES}" <<'PYEOF'
60	import sys
61	
62	elf = sys.argv[1]
63	objdump_file = sys.argv[2]
64	kanata_log = sys.argv[3]
65	log_cycles = int(sys.argv[4])
66	
67	from vsim import Vsim
68	import andes45
69	from utils import KanataLogger, parse_objdump_output
70	
71	# Set up simulator
72	simu = Vsim("ax45mpv_premium")
73	print(f"AndesCycle: {simu.get_version()}")
74	simu.load_elf(elf)
75	simu.reset()
76	
77	# Cycle-by-cycle pipeline logging from the start
78	kanata = KanataLogger(open(kanata_log, "w"))
79	with open(objdump_file, "r") as fin:
80	    kanata.code_map = parse_objdump_output(fin.read())
81	
82	has_vpu = True  # ax45mpv has VPU
83	recorders = [andes45.PipelineModeler(simu.cpu, kanata, has_vpu)]
84	
85	for cy in range(log_cycles):
86	    if simu.get_exits() >= 0:
87	        print(f"[vsim] Program exited after {cy} logged cycles")
88	        break
89	    for rec in recorders:
90	        rec.record(cy)
91	    simu.run(1)
92	    kanata.advance_cycle(1)
93	
94	kanata.flush_all()
95	print(f"[done] Logged {cy+1} cycles")
96	PYEOF
97	
98	RC=$?
99	if [ ${RC} -ne 0 ]; then
100	    echo "RESULT: VSIM_ERROR (exit ${RC})" >&2
101	    exit 1
102	fi
103	
104	echo ""
105	echo "--- vsim kernel profile ---"
106	echo "  Pipeline log: ${KANATA_LOG}"
107	echo "  Objdump:      ${OBJDUMP_FILE}"
108	echo "  Log size:     $(wc -l < "${KANATA_LOG}") lines"
109	echo "---"

125	#

127	# (no GDB load), no nds_pfcounter.h (we use raw csrr mcycle/minstret), and
128	# stdout goes through HTIF via libgloss. Cycle counts are RTL-cycle-accurate
129	# but very slow (~9 kHz) -- which is why we extract just the hot inner kernel
130	# instead of running the whole libnn perf test.

135	# vendor extensions (nds.vd4dots, nds.lea.h, etc.). -O1 matches the
136	# vsim-demo/acervv example -- the kernel.s is hand-emitted so the C
137	# wrapper's optimization level barely matters.
138	# Why rv64gc_zve32x_xandes (not the more permissive rv64gcv): with full `v`
139	# enabled, GCC emits e64 m8 vle64.v inside libc helpers (e.g. _fstat ->
140	# SYSCALL_2 path) and our vsim build (sim_ax45mpv_premium) traps

1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# Hot inner-loop detector wrapper.
3	#
4	# Runs an ELF under user-mode QEMU with the hotloops plugin and prints
5	# a sorted table of detected backward branches:
6	#
7	#   iters | start_pc -> end_pc | function:offset
8	#
9	# Usage:
10	#   scripts/hotloops.sh [opts] <elf> [--] [prog args...]
11	#
12	# Options:
13	#   --arch <riscv32|riscv64|auto>   Target arch (default: auto)
14	#   --qemu <path>                   Override qemu binary
15	#   --plugin <path>                 Override plugin .so
16	#   --limit <N>                     Top-N loops to print (default 50)
17	#   --min-iters <N>                 Hot-loop threshold; drop loops with
18	#                                   fewer than N iterations (default 1)
19	#   --objdump <path>                Override objdump (resolves symbols)
20	#   --addr2line <path>              Override addr2line (resolves source lines)
21	#   --no-source                     Disable source file:line resolution
22	#   --raw                           Print raw plugin output, no formatting
23	#   -h | --help                     Show help
24	
25	set -euo pipefail
26	
27	SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd -- "$(dirname -- "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
28	QEMU_ROOT="$(cd -- "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"
29	
30	ARCH="auto"
31	QEMU=""
32	PLUGIN=""
33	LIMIT=50
34	MIN_ITERS=1
35	OBJDUMP=""
36	ADDR2LINE=""
37	NO_SOURCE=0
38	RAW=0
39	
40	usage() { sed -n '2,20p' "$0"; exit 0; }
41	
42	# --- arg parsing ---
43	ELF=""
44	PROG_ARGS=()
45	while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
46	    case "$1" in
47	        --arch)    ARCH="$2"; shift 2 ;;
48	        --qemu)    QEMU="$2"; shift 2 ;;
49	        --plugin)  PLUGIN="$2"; shift 2 ;;
50	        --limit)   LIMIT="$2"; shift 2 ;;
51	        --min-iters) MIN_ITERS="$2"; shift 2 ;;
52	        --objdump) OBJDUMP="$2"; shift 2 ;;
53	        --addr2line) ADDR2LINE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
54	        --no-source) NO_SOURCE=1; shift ;;
55	        --raw)     RAW=1; shift ;;
56	        -h|--help) usage ;;
57	        --) shift; PROG_ARGS+=("$@"); break ;;
58	        -*) echo "unknown option: $1" >&2; exit 2 ;;
59	        *)
60	            if [[ -z "$ELF" ]]; then ELF="$1"; else PROG_ARGS+=("$1"); fi
61	            shift ;;
62	    esac
63	done
64	
65	[[ -n "$ELF" ]] || { echo "error: ELF path required" >&2; exit 2; }
66	[[ -f "$ELF" ]] || { echo "error: not found: $ELF" >&2; exit 2; }
67	
68	# --- arch detection ---
69	if [[ "$ARCH" == "auto" ]]; then
70	    file_out="$(file -L -- "$ELF" 2>/dev/null || true)"
71	    case "$file_out" in
72	        *"ELF 64-bit"*"RISC-V"*)  ARCH=riscv64 ;;
73	        *"ELF 32-bit"*"RISC-V"*)  ARCH=riscv32 ;;
74	        *) echo "error: cannot auto-detect arch (not RISC-V?), pass --arch" >&2; exit 2 ;;
75	    esac
76	fi
77	
78	# --- locate qemu + plugin ---
79	if [[ -z "$QEMU" ]]; then
80	    QEMU="${QEMU_ROOT}/build/qemu-${ARCH}"
81	fi
82	[[ -x "$QEMU" ]] || { echo "error: qemu not found/executable: $QEMU" >&2; exit 2; }
83	
84	if [[ -z "$PLUGIN" ]]; then
85	    PLUGIN="${QEMU_ROOT}/build/contrib/plugins/libhotloops.so"
86	fi
87	[[ -f "$PLUGIN" ]] || { echo "error: plugin not built: $PLUGIN" >&2; exit 2; }
88	
89	# --- run ---
90	LOG="$(mktemp -t hotloops.XXXXXX.log)"
91	trap 'rm -f "$LOG"' EXIT
92	
93	"$QEMU" \
94	    -plugin "${PLUGIN},limit=${LIMIT},min_iters=${MIN_ITERS}" \
95	    -d plugin -D "$LOG" \
96	    -- "$ELF" "${PROG_ARGS[@]}" >/dev/null
97	
98	if [[ "$RAW" -eq 1 ]]; then
99	    cat "$LOG"
100	    exit 0
101	fi
102	
103	# --- symbol table from objdump (best effort) ---
104	if [[ -z "$OBJDUMP" ]]; then
105	    for cand in llvm-objdump riscv64-unknown-elf-objdump \
106	                riscv32-unknown-elf-objdump objdump; do
107	        if command -v "$cand" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
108	            OBJDUMP="$cand"
109	            break
110	        fi
111	    done
112	fi
113	
114	SYMS="$(mktemp -t hotloops_syms.XXXXXX)"
115	LINES="$(mktemp -t hotloops_lines.XXXXXX)"
116	trap 'rm -f "$LOG" "$SYMS" "$LINES"' EXIT
117	if [[ -n "$OBJDUMP" ]]; then
118	    # Capture FUNC symbols: addr size type name
119	    "$OBJDUMP" -t "$ELF" 2>/dev/null \
120	        | awk 'tolower($0) ~ /[ \t]f[ \t]/ && NF>=5 {
121	                  addr=strtonum("0x" $1);
122	                  size=strtonum("0x" $(NF-1));
123	                  name=$NF;
124	                  if (size>0) print addr, size, name;
125	              }' \
126	        | sort -n -k1,1 > "$SYMS" || true
127	fi
128	
129	# --- locate addr2line for source line resolution (DWARF) ---
130	if [[ "$NO_SOURCE" -eq 0 && -z "$ADDR2LINE" ]]; then
131	    for cand in llvm-addr2line addr2line \
132	                riscv64-unknown-elf-addr2line riscv32-unknown-elf-addr2line \
133	                riscv64-linux-gnu-addr2line; do
134	        if command -v "$cand" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
135	            ADDR2LINE="$cand"
136	            break
137	        fi
138	    done
139	fi
140	
141	# Pre-collect all PCs from the log and batch-resolve to file:line.
142	# LINES file format: <hex-pc> <file:line>
143	if [[ "$NO_SOURCE" -eq 0 && -n "$ADDR2LINE" ]]; then
144	    pcs="$(awk -F',' '/^0x/ {gsub(/[ \t]/,"",$1); gsub(/[ \t]/,"",$2); print $1; print $2}' "$LOG" | sort -u)"
145	    if [[ -n "$pcs" ]]; then
146	        # addr2line: -e <elf> -f -s -C reads addrs on stdin, prints
147	        # alternating function/file:line; we ask for file:line only via -p.
148	        out="$(printf '%s\n' $pcs | "$ADDR2LINE" -e "$ELF" -s 2>/dev/null || true)"
149	        paste <(printf '%s\n' $pcs) <(printf '%s\n' "$out") > "$LINES" || true
150	    fi
151	fi
152	
153	# --- format ---
154	python3 - "$LOG" "$SYMS" "$LINES" "$LIMIT" "$MIN_ITERS" <<'PY'
155	import sys, re
156	
157	log_path = sys.argv[1]
158	syms_path = sys.argv[2]
159	lines_path = sys.argv[3]
160	limit = int(sys.argv[4])
161	min_iters = int(sys.argv[5])
162	
163	syms = []
164	try:
165	    with open(syms_path) as f:
166	        for line in f:
167	            parts = line.split()
168	            if len(parts) >= 3:
169	                addr = int(parts[0])
170	                size = int(parts[1])
171	                name = " ".join(parts[2:])
172	                syms.append((addr, size, name))
173	except FileNotFoundError:
174	    pass
175	
176	def resolve(pc):
177	    for addr, size, name in syms:
178	        if addr <= pc < addr + size:
179	            return f"{name}+0x{pc-addr:x}"
180	    return "?"
181	
182	src_map = {}
183	try:
184	    with open(lines_path) as f:
185	        for line in f:
186	            parts = line.rstrip("\n").split("\t", 1)
187	            if len(parts) == 2:
188	                pc_str, src = parts
189	                src = src.strip()
190	                if src and not src.startswith("??"):
191	                    try:
192	                        src_map[int(pc_str, 16)] = src
193	                    except ValueError:
194	                        pass
195	except FileNotFoundError:
196	    pass
197	
198	def src_line(pc):
199	    return src_map.get(pc, "")
200	
201	rows = []
202	with open(log_path) as f:
203	    for line in f:
204	        m = re.match(r"\s*(0x[0-9a-fA-F]+)\s*,\s*(0x[0-9a-fA-F]+)\s*,\s*(\d+)", line)
205	        if m:
206	            rows.append((int(m.group(1), 16),
207	                         int(m.group(2), 16),
208	                         int(m.group(3))))
209	
210	if not rows:
211	    sys.stderr.write("hotloops: no backward edges observed\n")
212	    sys.exit(0)
213	
214	rows = [r for r in rows if r[2] >= min_iters]
215	rows.sort(key=lambda r: -r[2])
216	rows = rows[:limit]
217	
218	if not rows:
219	    sys.stderr.write(f"hotloops: no loops with iters >= {min_iters}\n")
220	    sys.exit(0)
221	
222	print(f"# hot loops (iters >= {min_iters}), sorted desc, top {len(rows)}")
223	
224	have_src = bool(src_map)
225	
226	w_iter = max(len("iters"), max(len(str(r[2])) for r in rows))
227	w_pc = 18  # 0x + 16 hex
228	w_sym = max(len("location"),
229	            max(len(f"{resolve(r[0])} -> {resolve(r[1])}") for r in rows))
230	
231	if have_src:
232	    def srcpair(s, e):
233	        a, b = src_line(s), src_line(e)
234	        if a and b and a == b:
235	            return a
236	        return f"{a or '?'} -> {b or '?'}"
237	    w_src = max(len("source"),
238	                max(len(srcpair(r[0], r[1])) for r in rows))
239	    print(f"{'iters':>{w_iter}} | {'start_pc':<{w_pc}} -> {'end_pc':<{w_pc}} | "
240	          f"{'location':<{w_sym}} | source")
241	    print("-" * (w_iter + 3 + w_pc + 4 + w_pc + 3 + w_sym + 3 + w_src))
242	    for s, e, c in rows:
243	        loc = f"{resolve(s)} -> {resolve(e)}"
244	        print(f"{c:>{w_iter}} | 0x{s:016x} -> 0x{e:016x} | "
245	              f"{loc:<{w_sym}} | {srcpair(s, e)}")
246	else:
247	    print(f"{'iters':>{w_iter}} | {'start_pc':<{w_pc}} -> {'end_pc':<{w_pc}} | location")
248	    print("-" * (w_iter + 3 + w_pc + 4 + w_pc + 3 + w_sym))
249	    for s, e, c in rows:
250	        loc = f"{resolve(s)} -> {resolve(e)}"
251	        print(f"{c:>{w_iter}} | 0x{s:016x} -> 0x{e:016x} | {loc}")
252	PY
253

2	################################################################################
3	# Parse config.toml and export CFG_* variables for use by other scripts.
4	#
5	# Usage (source with explicit path):
6	#   source parse_config.sh /path/to/config.toml
7	#
8	# Usage (source with env var):
9	#   CONFIG_TOML=/path/to/config.toml source parse_config.sh
10	#
11	# Usage (source with default - resolves to ../config.toml next to this script):
12	#   source parse_config.sh
13	#
14	# Exported variables (all derived from config.toml - no hardcoding):
15	#
16	#   [repo]
17	#     CFG_LIBNN_ROOT        - libnn source root (absolute)
18	#     CFG_STATE_FILE        - absolute path to state.json
19	#     CFG_WORK_DIR          - absolute path to work directory
20	#
21	#   [toolchain]
22	#     CFG_TOOLCHAIN_BIN     - toolchain bin directory
23	#     CFG_TOOLCHAIN_PREFIX  - toolchain prefix (e.g. riscv64-elf-)
24	#     CFG_CC                - full path to gcc                 (derived)
25	#     CFG_AR, CFG_RANLIB    - full paths to ar, ranlib         (derived)
26	#     CFG_OBJDUMP, CFG_GDB  - full paths to objdump, gdb       (derived)
27	#
28	#   [target]
29	#     CFG_CPU               - target CPU name
30	#     CFG_FPGA_CONFIG       - absolute path to .inc hardware file
31	#     CFG_UARCH_REF         - absolute path to uarch doc
32	#     CFG_INTRINSIC_API     - absolute path to intrinsic api doc
33	#     CFG_CFLAGS_BASE       - space-joined base CFLAGS
34	#     CFG_CFLAGS_PERF       - space-joined extra CFLAGS for perf build
35	#     CFG_CFLAGS_VERIFY     - space-joined extra CFLAGS for verify build
36	#     CFG_DEFINES_DEFINED   - newline-joined list of defined macros
37	#     CFG_DEFINES_UNDEFINED - newline-joined list of undefined macros
38	#
39	#   [qemu]
40	#     CFG_QEMU_BIN          - QEMU binary path
41	#     CFG_QEMU_MACHINE      - QEMU machine name (-M argument)
42	#     CFG_QEMU_CPU          - QEMU CPU string (-cpu argument)
43	#     CFG_QEMU_CONFIG       - absolute path to QEMU device config file
44	#     CFG_QEMU_VLEN         - QEMU vector length
45	#     CFG_QEMU_TIMEOUT      - QEMU timeout seconds
46	#
47	#   [fpga]
48	#     CFG_FPGA_HOST         - FPGA board hostname
49	#     CFG_FPGA_PORT         - FPGA board port
50	#     CFG_FPGA_TIMEOUT      - FPGA timeout seconds
51	#     CFG_FPGA_LD_SCRIPT    - absolute path to FPGA linker script
52	#     CFG_FPGA_SIM_WRAPPER  - absolute path to GDB wrapper script
53	#     CFG_FPGA_RESET_FLAG   - absolute path to First_Reset_done flag file
54	#
55	#   [limits]
56	#     CFG_MAX_ROUNDS        - max optimization rounds per function
57	#     CFG_VALIDATE_CACHE_H  - validate cache TTL in hours
58	#
59	#   [meta_harness]
60	#     CFG_TRACE_ENABLED     - "1" if trace emission is on, "0" if off
61	################################################################################
62

64	_pc_script_dir="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"

69	    return 1 2>/dev/null || exit 1
70	fi

73	import sys, tomllib, shlex, os

1	# Kanata Loop-Window Pipeline Dump
2	
3	Restrict a Kanata or Clarity pipeline dump to a chosen number of iterations of one specific loop body, after a configurable warmup. The simulator exits as soon as the window closes and reports per-window IPC.
4	
5	## When to use it
6	
7	- Inspect steady-state pipeline behavior of a hot loop in Konata.
8	- Get IPC for the loop body alone, ignoring warmup and tail effects.
9	- Produce a small, shareable pipeline trace instead of a multi-GB full-run log.
10	
11	## Flags
12	
13	All flags are optional and only take effect with `--kanata` or `--clarity`. `--kanata-loop-start` and `--kanata-loop-end` must be specified together.
14	
15	| Flag | Argument | Default | Meaning |
16	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
17	| `--kanata-loop-start`  | `<pc>` | -  | Start PC of the loop body. Reported in the summary; not used to gate iterations. Hex (`0x...`) or decimal accepted. |
18	| `--kanata-loop-end`    | `<pc>` | -  | End PC of the loop body. Each retire of this PC counts as one iteration. |
19	| `--kanata-loop-skip`   | `<N>`  | 0  | Warmup iterations to ignore before the window opens. `0` records from the first iteration. |
20	| `--kanata-loop-record` | `<X>`  | 1  | Iterations to capture after warmup. Must be >= 1. Simulator exits when this many iterations have retired. |
21	
22	## How iterations are counted
23	
24	An iteration is counted only when the instruction at `--kanata-loop-end` __retires__. Mispredicted or flushed fetches of that PC do not advance the counter, so the recorded window is always exactly `record` complete iterations of the loop body.
25	
26	## Picking the start/end PCs
27	
28	Use `objdump -d` on your ELF and pick:
29	
30	- `start` = first instruction of the loop body (typically the branch target / loop label).
31	- `end`   = last instruction of the loop body (typically the back-branch, or the instruction immediately before it if you only want taken iterations).
32	
33	Both PCs must be reachable in the simulated execution. If `end` is never retired, the window never opens and the simulator runs to completion normally.
34	
35	## Output
36	
37	While the gate is closed (warmup), no `I` / `C` / `R` records are emitted, but the internal cycle counter still advances so timing inside the window is accurate. When the window closes, the logger writes to spdlog:
38	
39	```
40	Kanata loop window [pc 0x...start..0x...end]: <iters> iters, <insns> insns, <cycles> cycles, IPC=<ipc>
41	```
42	
43	and the simulator exits with status 0. The dump file contains a self-consistent Kanata trace for those iterations only and can be opened directly in Konata.
44	
45	## Example
46	
47	```
48	vsim --elf bench.elf \
49	     --kanata bench.kanata \
50	     --kanata-loop-start 0x80001234 \
51	     --kanata-loop-end   0x80001260 \
52	     --kanata-loop-skip  100 \
53	     --kanata-loop-record 8
54	```
55	
56	Skips the first 100 iterations, records the next 8, prints the IPC summary, and exits. `bench.kanata` contains those 8 iterations only.
57	
58	## Constraints
59	
60	- `--kanata-loop-*` requires `--kanata` or `--clarity`; otherwise startup fails.
61	- `--kanata-loop-start` and `--kanata-loop-end` must both be present or both absent.
62	- `--kanata-loop-record` must be >= 1.
63	- `--kanata` and `--clarity` remain mutually exclusive.
64
